FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study with baseline ages 9-12 (n=4,641); longitudinal design with Year 1 and Year 2 follow-ups; methods section confirms Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) examining reciprocal predictive relationships between sleep duration/problems, SMA, and psychopathology; discovery and replication samples analyzed.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2024; no indication of preprint or duplicate status.

FT_IC2.evidence: SMA (screen media activity) measured as hours spent on non-school-related activities including TV shows, videos, gaming, texting, video chatting, and social networking sites. SMA functions as both IV and DV in bidirectional CLPMs. Paired with individual-trait-level outcomes: sleep duration/problems (behavioral) and psychopathology symptoms (internalizing/externalizing mental health).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results for SMA↔individual-trait-level relationships: bidirectional SMA-sleep associations (β = -0.10 [95%CI: -0.16, -0.03], p=0.004; β = -0.11 [95%CI: -0.18, -0.05], p<0.001); externalizing symptoms predicting SMA (β = 0.07 [95%CI: 0.01, 0.13], p=0.014); indirect effect of baseline thalamus-PFC-brainstem covariation on SMA via externalizing behavior (indirect effect = 0.032 [95%CI: 0.003, 0.067], p=0.030).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential CLPM analysis with reported β coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values across discovery and replication samples; findings replicated in validation set.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline to Year 1/Year 2). SMA functions as both IV and DV in bidirectional CLPMs (not DV-only). Paper explicitly examines reciprocal relationships with SMA as IV predicting future sleep and psychopathology, and sleep/psychopathology predicting future SMA.
notes: FT-IC2 conceptually qualifies: SMA is measured as time on phone/screen-based media activities (streaming, gaming, social networking) paired with individual-trait-level variables. Cascade test: all criteria MET; FT-EC7 does not apply → INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 10,828 youth (47.8% female) enrolled in Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; baseline mean age 9.9 years, one-year follow-up mean age 10.9 years; NIH-funded, multi-site cohort.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal study examining bidirectional associations using one-year follow-up data; statistical analyses include gender-stratified models with dichotomized behavioral variables and outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in The Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024; full original research article.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as youth self-reported hours on weekdays and weekend days, dichotomized as >2 vs. ≤2 hours/day. Examined as IV predicting internalizing symptoms and as outcome of baseline internalizing status. Individual-trait-level outcomes: internalizing symptoms (mental health) measured via CBCL subscales including withdrawn/depressed, anxious/depressed, and somatic symptom subscales.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results for screen time↔internalizing symptom associations: in females, baseline weekend screen time associated with increased withdrawn/depressed symptoms one year later (OR 1.6, 95% CI 1.1–2.2); baseline sleep duration protective against withdrawn/depressed (OR 0.6, 95% CI 0.4–0.8) and somatic complaints (OR 0.8, 95% CI 0.6–0.97).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with odds ratios, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values; gender-stratified models examining associations between baseline behavioral variables and one-year follow-up internalizing symptoms.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline to 1-year follow-up). Screen time functions as both IV (predicting future internalizing symptoms) and outcome of baseline internalizing status (bidirectional). Paper explicitly states unidirectional behavior-preceding-symptoms pattern with screen time as IV predicting symptom outcomes.
notes: FT-IC2 qualifies: screen time measured as hours/day and paired with individual-trait-level mental health outcome (internalizing symptoms via CBCL). Cascade test: all criteria MET; FT-EC7 does not apply (bidirectional with SM-as-IV role) → INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; multi-site longitudinal study of nationally representative sample of U.S. youth; baseline 2017–2018 (mean age 9.9 years), one-year follow-up 2018–2019; N=10,544 total analytic sample across isotemporal and CoDA analyses.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis with isotemporal substitution models and compositional data analysis (CoDA); sex-stratified models examining baseline behaviors predicting follow-up BMI z-score.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity, 2024; open-access research article.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured in six forms: streaming/television shows/movies, gaming, and socializing collapsed into three categories (streaming, gaming, socializing). Methods explicitly separate different forms of screen time (not generic 'internet use' or composite without breakdown). Screen time functions as IV predicting BMI. BMI is an individual-trait-level physical health variable (body mass index z-score at follow-up).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results from isotemporal substitution analyses: in females, replacing 30 min socializing with physical activity (β = -0.03 [95% CI -0.05, -0.002]); replacing 30 min streaming with physical activity (β = -0.03 [95% CI -0.05, -0.01]); replacing 30 min gaming with physical activity (β = -0.03 [95% CI -0.06, -0.01]). In males, CoDA results: greater baseline socializing associated with higher BMI z-score (β = 0.05 [95% CI 0.02, 0.08]).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential longitudinal analysis with β coefficients, 95% confidence intervals; isotemporal substitution and CoDA models examined associations between baseline screen time and follow-up BMI z-score; sex-stratified analyses performed.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline 2017–2018 to follow-up 2018–2019). Screen time functions exclusively as IV in all analyses, predicting BMI z-score as DV. Paper does not analyze SM/phone as outcome/DV; FT-EC7 requires SM as DV-only (not IV-only) for exclusion to apply.
notes: FT-IC2 qualifies: screen time measured by form (streaming, gaming, socializing categories) with per-modality breakdown, paired with individual-trait-level health outcome (BMI). Cascade test: all criteria MET; FT-EC7 does not apply (SM is IV, not DV) → INCLUDE.
